perturbation.original_text: 7.1 Without limiting the generality of any other clause in this NDA, either Party may terminate this NDA immediatel\ b\ giYing the other Part\, thirt\ (30) da\s¶ Zritten notice if the other Party: (a) is in breach o f the terms and conditions of this NDA and such breach is not remedied within thirty (30) days of a written notification from the non- defaulting Party requiring the same to be remedied;
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 Without limiting the generality of any other clause in this NDA, either Party may terminate this NDA immediately by giving the other Party, fourteen (14) days’ written notice if the other Party: (a) is in breach of the terms and conditions of this NDA and such breach is not remedied within fourteen (14) days of a written notification from the non-defaulting Party requiring the same to be remedied;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for breach remedy to 14 days. This may be unreasonably short depending on the complexity of the breach and the nature of the agreement, especially in a Malaysian context where laws imply reasonable timeframes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of Malaysian contract law, specifically Section 56 of the Contracts Act 1950
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 56 of the Contracts Act 1950 implies that performance of a contract must be within a reasonable time if no time is specified.  Reducing the remedy period to 14 days may be considered unreasonable in certain breach scenarios, conflicting with the principle of 'reasonable time' for performance.
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 days to 14 days for remedying a breach potentially contradicts the implied requirement of a 'reasonable time' as indicated by the Law Explanation, which references Section 56 of the Contracts Act 1950. Although specific legal snippets are absent to confirm the precise wording and application of Section 56, the perturbation explanation supports the view that 14 days may be unreasonably short in certain breach scenarios, making the contract potentially unenforceable under the Act's principle of 'reasonable time'.
